assumptions: The analysis is for consolidated financial statements.
The intragroup dividend receivable is already recognised and is denominated in a foreign currency.
The fact pattern does not state whether the related foreign exchange gains or losses are fully eliminated on consolidation, so eligibility is conditional on that point.
No facts indicate that the group is seeking to hedge a net investment itself rather than the recognised dividend receivable.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedged-item prohibition
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally be exposures with parties external to the reporting entity. An intragroup dividend receivable is therefore not eligible under the general rule.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent an explicit foreign-currency exception, hedge accounting cannot be documented on the intragroup receivable in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can be hedged items in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A recognised dividend receivable may fit this exception if it is treated as such a monetary item.
conditions: The dividend receivable is a recognised intragroup monetary item
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
Formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met under IFRS 9 paragraph 6.4.1
approaches.practical_implication: The group may document hedge accounting for the FX risk only if the consolidation-level exception is clearly met and evidenced.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when the FX exposure is not fully eliminated.
Paragraphs 6.4.1 and 6.5.1 require formal designation, documentation, and satisfaction of hedge effectiveness criteria.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss. The stated facts concern an already recognised dividend receivable, not a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: The forecast-transaction exception is not the relevant hedge-accounting route for a receivable already on the books.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 6.3.6 and B6.3.5 permit certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged if the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph 6.3.3 requires a forecast transaction used as a hedged item to be highly probable.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 deals with hedging the foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. That is conceptually different from hedging a recognised intragroup dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: This route is relevant only if the documented hedge is of the foreign operation’s net investment, not of the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10–12 define the hedged risk and hedged item in a net investment hedge as FX exposure on net assets of a foreign operation.
Paragraphs 13–15 add constraints on single qualification in consolidation and require IFRS 9 documentation and effectiveness conditions to be met.
